content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street ♦ Suite 620 ♦ Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 ♦ Fax (916) 322-0886

May 24, 2012


City of San Diego Ethics Committee
Stacey Fulhorst
Executive Director
1010 Second Avenue, Suite 1530
San Diego, CA 92101

Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No. I-12-045

Dear Ms. Fulhorst:

This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature and not limited to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.²

Please note that the Commission does not provide advice on bodies of law outside the confines of the Act. Thus, we offer no opinion on the application of other incompatible activities and conflict-of-interest laws that may apply including, but not limited to, common law conflict of interest and Government Code Section 1090.

QUESTION

May a planning commissioner contact other city employees regarding a land use decision that may potentially come before the Planning Commission?


¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

File No. I-12-045
Page No. 2


CONCLUSION

Generally, a planning commissioner may not appear before or communicate with a staff
member of another city department, on behalf of a client, if the staff member is assigned or
involved in a project that is or will be brought before the Planning Commission, and it is
reasonably foreseeable that the Planning Commission's decision regarding the project will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the commissioner's economic interests.

FACTS

You are the Executive Director for the City of San Diego Ethics Commission and seek
general assistance regarding the Act's conflict-of-interest provision as applied to
communications or appearances by planning commissioners before other city staff, including
staff assigned to the Planning Commission, regarding projects that may come before the
Planning Commission. You have provided the following facts for our consideration:

• The Development Services Department (the "DSD") manages and oversees development
  projects in the City of San Diego (the "City").

• The DSD provides clerical support to the City's Planning Commission, and DSD
  managerial employees (including both Project Managers and Planners) attend Planning
  Commission meetings and hearings.

• The Planning Commission does not have appointment or budgetary control over the DSD
  or any other City staff.

• The City's land use decisions fall under one of five distinct processes.

• Process One projects do not come before the Planning Commission.

• Process Two and Three projects are appealable to the Planning Commission for a final
  decision.

• Process Four projects are heard by the Planning Commission but the Planning
  Commission's decision is appealable to the City Council.

• Process Five projects are heard by the Planning Commission for a recommendation to the
  City Council.

• Approximately five-percent of Process Two and Three projects are actually appealed to
  the Planning Commission.

File No. I-12-045
Page No. 3

• DSD Senior Planners may serve as Hearing Officers for Process Three hearings, which
may be appealed to the Planning Commission.

• The City is also represented by a DSD Project Manager or Planner during Process Three
hearings. Generally, the DSD Project Manager or Planner representing the City at a
Process Three hearing will be the same DSD Project Manager or Planner representing the
City if the project is appealed to the Planning Commission.

• The City recognizes a number of community planning groups ("CPG"), which are private
organizations that advise the City on land use issues. CPGs generally provide
recommendations on some (but not all) Process 2 and 5 projects. CPGs occasionally
weigh in on Process One projects as well.

• DSD managerial employees may occasionally attend CPG meetings and may provide an
overview of a proposed project to the CPG, gather information and forward it to other
City staff, and gather information to be used in presentations to the Planning
Commission.

ANALYSIS

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their
duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically,
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a
financial interest. An official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the official's economic interests. (Section 87103;
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision.
(Regulation 18700(b)(1)-(8).)

Your questions generally revolve around the applicability of the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act, if a planning commissioner contacts another city employee, who is not
with the Planning Commission, regarding a land use decision that may potentially come before
the Planning Commission assuming an otherwise disqualifying conflict of interest. We therefore
limit our analysis to Step Two of the eight-step standard analysis -- the determination of whether
a planning commissioner is making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental
decision when appearing before or communicating with other city employees.

Step Two: Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision.

The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only when a public official "make[s],
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his [or her] official position to influence a

File No. I-12-045
Page No. 4

governmental decision in which he [or she] knows or has reason to know [he or she] has a
financial interest." (Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) In other words, an official is not
prohibited from contacting an agency under the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions if the
official is not making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision. The
Commission has adopted a series of regulations defining "making," "participating in making,"
and "influencing" a governmental decision. (Regulations 18702-18702.3.)

Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "makes a governmental decision"
when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter,
appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into
any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.1(a).)

Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "participates in
making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and
without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes
recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100;
Regulation 18702.2.)

Influencing a Governmental Decision: There are two rules that address whether a public
official is using or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision. The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before the public
official's own agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public
official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) In these cases, if "the official contacts, or appears
before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency" then he or she is attempting to influence a governmental decision. This includes, but is
not limited to, "appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer."

The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an agency
other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the
budgetary control of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).) Under this rule, the
official cannot act or purport "to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her agency to
any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency" to influence a decision that will have
a material financial effect on his or her economic interests.

While a public official may not contact staff of his or her own agency for the purpose of
influencing a decision in which he or she has a financial interest, including contacts on behalf of
a business entity, client, or customer, a public official may contact other staff so long as the
official makes it clear that he or she is not purporting to speak on behalf of his or her agency.
Because your questions generally pertain to a planning commissioner contacting another city
employee regarding a land use decision, the threshold determination that must be made is
whether a planning commissioner is using his or her position to influence his or her own agency
or an agency other the official's own agency in contacting another city employee.

File No. I-12-045
Page No. 5

In this regard, we previously advised in the Farrell Advice Letter, No. I-03-121:

"[I]t is important to realize that staff does not exist in a vacuum; it is not a generic
entity with its own identity. Rather, staff is a component of the agency to which it
is assigned. An "agency" includes the staff assigned exclusively to a public
official's agency (Larmore Advice Letter, No. A-00-275; Martello Advice Letter,
No. A-85-190) as well as staff that are shared between that and another agency
(West Advice Letter, No. A-88-413)."

Accordingly, we concluded that a member of a design review board could not meet or
communicate with a staff member of another city department on behalf a client if (1) the staff
member is assigned or involved in a project, analysis, presentation, or other matter that is or will
be brought before the design review board, and (2) it is reasonably foreseeable that the design
review board's decision regarding the item in which the staff member is involved will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the member's economic interests. In short, when staff
is shared for a project which is, or will be, before the official's agency, meeting or otherwise
communicating with the shared staff regarding the project is an appearance or communication
with the official's own agency. (Farrell Advice Letter, supra.)

Applied to your facts, a planning commissioner may not appear before or communicate
with a staff member of another city department, on behalf a client, if the staff member is
assigned or involved in a project that may potentially be brought before the Planning
Commission (including but not limited to a staff member who advises or provides analysis to the
Planning Commission regarding the project) and it is reasonably foreseeable that the Planning
Commission's decision regarding the project in which the staff member is involved will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the commissioner's economic interests.

1. Is a DSD employee involved in Planning Commission matters considered a "member,
officer, employee, or consultant of the Planning Commission?

Yes. A DSD employee involved in Planning Commission matters is considered a
component of the Planning Commission itself. (Farrell Advice Letter, supra.) An appearance
or communication before a DSD employee by a planning commissioner regarding a project that
is or will be before the Planning Commission is considered an appearance or contact with the
official's own agency. A planning commissioner is prohibited from making an appearance or
communication before his or her own agency if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable
material financial effect on any economic interest the official may have.

2. May a planning commissioner contact DSD clerical staff assigned to the Planning
Commission regarding a client's project? What if the communication is solely related to a
ministerial issue?

Yes, but only with regard to Process One projects or solely ministerial matters. It is
foreseeable that Process Two through Five projects will come before the Planning Commission

File No. I-12-045
Page No. 6

for review or a recommendation. An appearance or communication before DSD staff regarding
any Process Two through Five projects will be considered an appearance or communication with
the official's own agency. Accordingly, a planning commissioner is prohibited from making the
appearance or communication if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on any economic interest the official may have. However, an appearance or
communication before DSD staff regarding a Process One project, which will not come before
the Planning Commission, is not an appearance or communication before an official's own
agency. Accordingly, a planning commissioner may appear before or communicate with DSD
staff regarding a Process One project so long as the commissioner does not purport to be acting
on behalf of the Planning Commission.

An official is not making or participating in making a government decision if the
official's actions are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical. (Regulation
18702.4(a)(1).) We have also previously advised that a council member may contact city staff to
"pose general questions which are ministerial in nature" (Torrance Advice Letter, No. A-94-084)
and that "the Act would not prohibit [a planning commissioner] from obtaining information
otherwise available to the public" (Gallagher Advice Letter, No. I-94-279). Accordingly, a
planning commissioner is not prohibited from communicating with a DSD clerical staff member,
even if assigned to the Planning Commission or a project before the Planning Commission, if the
matter is solely ministerial.³

3. May a planning commissioner contact DSD employees regarding a client's Process
One project?

Yes. See response to Question 2.

4. May a planning commissioner contact DSD employees regarding a client's Process
Two or Three project?

Yes, but only with regard to solely ministerial matters. See response to Question 2.

5. Is a DSD Senior Planner considered a member, officer, employee, or consultant of the
Planning Commission if the planner serves as a hearing officer for a Process Three project that
may be appealed to the Planning Commission?

Yes. A DSD Senior Planner that serves as a hearing officer for a Process Three project is
considered a component of the Planning Commission itself. (Farrell Advice Letter, supra.) An
appearance or communication by a planning commissioner regarding a Process Three Project,
which may be appealed to the Planning Commission, before a DSD Senior Planner that serves as
a hearing officer for the project is considered an appearance or contact with the planning
commissioner's own agency. A planning commissioner is prohibited from making the

_______________________
³ If you need additional assistance determining whether any particular communication is ministerial for
purposes of the Act, you should seek additional advice providing a description of the actual communication.

File No. I-12-045
Page No. 7

appearance or communication if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on any economic interest the official may have.

6. May a planning commissioner represent or assist a client at a Process Three project
hearing in which a DSD Senior Planner is serving as the hearing officer and the city is
represented by a DSD project manager who may later be involved in the matter if appealed to
the Planning Commission?

No. An appearance or communication by a planning commissioner regarding a Process
Three Project, which may be appealed to the Planning Commission, before a DSD Senior
Planner that serves as a hearing officer for the project or a DSD project manager representing the
city at the hearing is considered an appearance or contact with the planning commissioner's own
agency. A planning commissioner is prohibited from making the appearance or communication
if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic
interest the official may have.

7. May a planning commissioner contact a DSD employee regarding a client's project
after the Planning Commission has acted on the project and there is no possibility that the
matter will be remanded back to the Planning Commission?

Yes. In the Farrell Advice Letter, supra, we previously found that a member of a design
review board may communicate with shared city staff assigned to a project once the board had
rendered is final design review for the project. We reached this conclusion because an official is
not attempting to influence a decision by the official's agency if the official contacts shared city
staff after the official's agency has made a final decision. Similarly, a planning commissioner is
not attempting to influence the Planning Commission if the commissioner contacts a DSD
employee assigned to the project after the Planning Commission renders its final decision
regarding the project. Accordingly, once the Planning Commission has rendered its final
decision regarding a project, a planning commissioner may contact a DSD employee regarding
the project, even if the DSD employee is assigned to the project, so long as the commissioner
does not purport to be acting on behalf of the Planning Commission.

8. May a planning commissioner contact a DSD employee regarding a client's project if
the employee will not be involved in presenting that particular project to the Planning
Commission? Will the answer change if that employee is involved in presenting other projects to
the Planning Commission?

No. An appearance or communication by a planning commissioner regarding a project
that is or will be before the Planning Commission to a DSD employee is considered an
appearance or contact with the official's own agency because the DSD employee is considered
shared staff for that particular project, even if the DSD employee will not be presenting the
project to the Planning Commission. A planning commissioner is prohibited from making the
appearance or communication if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on any economic interest the official may have.

File No. 1-12-045
Page No. 8

9. Is a matter before a CPG, which may be appealed to the Planning Commission, a
governmental decision that is within or before the Planning Commission?

Yes. CPGs are recognized by the City and advise the City on land use issues.
Notwithstanding the fact that you have identified CPGs as private organizations, should
members of a CPG make, participate in making, or use their official positions to influence
governmental decisions, a CPG is an agency of the City. Conversely, if members of a CPG do
not make, participate in making or use their official positions to influence governmental
decisions, a CPG may be considered a purely advisory body. (See Barker Advice Letter, No. A-
11-241.)

While you have not provided sufficient information for us to determine whether any
particular CPG is a decision-making agency of the City or purely an advisory body, we find it
unnecessary to make this determination at this time. Even if a CPG is acting only as an advisory
body, CPG members or staff involved in making a recommendation regarding a project that may
come before the Planning Commission must be considered shared staff of the Planning
Commission. Thus, any appearance or communication before a CPG by a planning
commissioner regarding a project that may come before the Planning Commission would be
prohibited as an appearance or communication before the commissioner's own agency if the
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interest the
official may have.

10. May a planning commissioner make a presentation concerning a client's project to a
CPG if the CPG may make a recommendation on the project to the Planning Commission?

No. See response to Question 9.

11. If a planning commissioner can address a CPG on behalf of a client, may the
Planning Commission consider the minutes of the CPG meeting when reviewing the project?

See response to Question 9.

12. May a planning commissioner have discussions with DSD staff on behalf of a client
regarding early feasibility issues if the permit process for the project has yet to be determined?

No. Considering the Planning Commission's role in the Process Two through Five
projects, it is foreseeable that projects for which the permit process has yet to be determined will
eventually come before the Planning Commission. Thus, any appearance or communication by a
planning commissioner before DSD staff regarding a project for which the permit process has yet
to be determined would be prohibited as an appearance or communication before the
commissioner's own agency if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial
effect on any economic interest the official may have.

File No. I-12-045
Page No. 9


13. May a planning commissioner make a presentation concerning a client's project to a different City board if that board also receives staff support from DSD?

It depends. If the board is considering a project that may come before the Planning Commission, any appearance or communication by a planning commissioner before board staff regarding the project would be prohibited as an appearance or communication before the commissioner's own agency if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interest the official may have. However, if the project will not come before the Planning Commission, a planning commissioner may make a presentation concerning a client's project, even if the board receives staff support from the DSD, so long as the commissioner does not purport to be acting on behalf of the Planning Commission.

14. May a planning commissioner contact a DSD employee concerning a client's project that will be considered by a different city board if that DSD employee also works on Planning Commission matters?

It depends. If the project may come before the Planning Commission, any appearance or communication by a planning commissioner before a DSD employee regarding the project would be prohibited as an appearance or communication before the commissioner's own agency if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interest the official may have. If the project will not come before the Planning Commission, a planning commissioner may contact a DSD employee concerning a project that will be considered by a different city board, even if the DSD employee also works on Planning Commission matters, so long as the commissioner does not purport to be acting on behalf of the Planning Commission.

15. For Questions 13 and 14, is it relevant whether the project under consideration by the other board could potentially come before the Planning Commission?

Yes. See response to Questions 13 and 14.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: Brian G. Lau
Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

sections.question:
May a planning commissioner contact other city employees regarding a land use decision 
that may potentially come before the Planning Commission?2 Informal assistance does not provide the rcquestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c}(3).)
sections.conclusion:
Generally, a planning commissioner may not appear before or communicate with a staff 
member of another city department, on behalf of a client, if the staff member is assigned or 
involved in a project that is or will be brought before the Planning Commission, and it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the Planning Commission's decision regarding the project will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the commissioner's economic interests.
sections.facts:
You are the Executive Director for the City of San Diego Ethics Commission and seek 
general assistance regarding the Act's conflict-of-interest provision as applied to 
communications or appearances by planning commissioners before other city staff, including 
staff assigned to the Planning Commission, regarding projects that may come before the 
Planning Commission. You have provided the following facts for our consideration: 
• The Development Services Department (the "DSD") manages and oversees development 
projects in the City of San Diego (the "City"). 
• The DSD provides clerical support to the City's Planning Commission, and DSD 
managerial employees (including both Project Managers and Planners) attend Planning 
Commission meetings and hearings. 
• The Planning Commission does not have appointment or budgetary control over the DSD 
or any other City staff. 
• The City's land use decisions fall under one of five distinct processes. 
• Process One projects do not come before the Planning Commission. 
• Process Two and Three projects are appealable to the Planning Commission for a final 
decision. 
• Process Four projects are heard by the Planning Commission but the Planning 
Commission's decision is appealable to the City Council. 
• Process Five projects are heard by the Planning Commission for a recommendation to the 
City Council. 
• Approximately five-percent of Process Two and Three projects are actually appealed to 
the Planning Commission.  
• DSD Senior Planners may serve as Hearing Officers for Process Three hearings. which 
may be appealed to the Planning Commission. 
• The City is also represented by a DSD Project Manager or Planner during Process Three 
hearings. Generally, the DSD Project Manager or Planner representing the City at a 
Process Three hearing will be the same DSD Project Manager or Planner representing the 
City if the project is appealed to the Planning Commission. 
• The City recognizes a number of community planning groups ("CPG"), which are private 
organizations that advise the City on land use issues. CPGs generally provide 
recommendations on some (but not all) Process 2 and 5 projects. CPGs occasionally 
weigh in on Process One projects as well. 
• DSD managerial employees may occasionally attend CPG meetings and may provide an 
overview of a proposed project to the CPG, gather information and forward it to other 
City staff, and gather information to be used in presentations to the Planning 
Commission.
sections.analysis:
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their 
duties in an impartial manner. free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the 
financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Specifically, 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise 
using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a 
financial interest. An official has a ••financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the 
meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the governmental decision will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the official's economic interests. (Section 87103; 
Regulation 18700(a).) The Commission has adopted an eight-step standard analysis for deciding 
whether an individual has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision. 
(Regulation 18700(b)(l)-(8).) 
Your questions generally revolve around the applicability of the conflict-of-interest 
provisions of the Act, if a planning commissioner contacts another city employee, who is not 
with the Planning Commission, regarding a land use decision that may potentially come before 
the Planning Commission assuming an otherwise disqualifying conflict of interest. We therefore 
limit our analysis to Step Two of the eight-step standard analysis -- the determination of whether 
a pla.nning commissioner is making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental 
decision when appearing before or communicating with other city employees. 
Step Two: Making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision. 
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only when a public official "make[s], 
participate[s] in making, or in any way attempts to use his [or her] official position to influence a  
governmental decision in which he [ or she] knows or has reason to know he [ or she] has a 
financial interest." (Section 87100; Regulation 18700(b)(2).) In other words, an official is not 
prohibited from contacting an agency under the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions if the 
official is not making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision. The 
Commission has adopted a series of regulations defining "making," 
0participating in making," 
and "influencing" a governmental decision. (Regulations 18702-18702.3.) 
Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "makes a governmental decision" 
when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, 
appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into 
any contractual agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18702.l(a).) 
Participating in Making a Governmental Decision: A public official "participates in 
making a governmental decision" when, acting within the authority of his or her position and 
without significant substantive or intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes 
recommendations to the decision-maker regarding the governmental decision. (Section 87100; 
Regulation 18702.2.) 
Influencing a Governmental Decision: There are two rules that address whether a public 
official is using or attempting to use his or her official position to influence a governmental 
decision. The first rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before the public 
official's own agency or an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary control of the public 
official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(a).) In these cases, if .. the official contacts, or appears 
before, or otherwise attempts to influence, any member, officer, employee or consultant of the 
agency" then he or she is attempting to influence a governmental decision. This includes, but is 
not limited to, "appearances or contacts by the official on behalf of a business entity, client, or 
customer." 
The second rule applies when the governmental decision is within or before an agency 
other than the public official's own agency, or an agency appointed by or subject to the 
budgetary control of the public official's agency. (Regulation 18702.3(b).) Under this rule, the 
official cannot act or purport "to act on behalf of, or as the representative of, his or her agency to 
any member, officer, employee or consultant of an agency" to influence a decision that will have 
a material financial effect on his or her economic interests. 
While a public official may not contact staff of his or her own agency for the purpose of 
influencing a decision in which he or she has a financial interest, including contacts on behalf of 
a business entity, client, or customer, a public official may contact other staff so long as the 
official makes it clear that he or she is not purporting to speak on behalf of his or her agency. 
Because your questions generally pertain to a planning commissioner contacting another city 
employee regarding a land use decision, the threshold determination that must be made is 
whether a planning commissioner is using his or her position to influence his or her own agency 
or an agency other the official's own agency in contacting another city employee:  
In this regard, we previously advised in the Farrell Advice Letter, No. 1-03-121: 
"[l]t is important to realize that staff does not exist in a vacuum; it is not a generic 
entity with its own identity. Rather, staff is a component of the agency to which it 
is assigned. An .. agency" includes the staff assigned exclusively to a public 
official's agency (Lannore Advice Letter, No. A-00-275; Martello Advice Letter, 
No. A-85-190) as well as staff that are shared between that and another agency 
(West Advice Letter, No. A-88-413)." 
Accordingly, we concluded that a member of a design review board could not meet or 
communicate with a staff member of another city department on behalf a client if ( 1) the staff 
member is assigned or involved in a project, analysis, presentation, or other matter that is or will 
be brought before the design review board, and (2) it is reasonably foreseeable that the design 
review board's decision regarding the item in which the staff member is involved will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the member's economic interests. In short, when staff 
is shared for a project which is, or will be, before the official's agency, meeting or otherwise 
communicating with the shared staff regarding the project is an appearance or communication 
with the official's own agency. (Farrell Advice Letter, supra.) 
Applied to your facts, a planning commissioner may not appear before or communicate 
with a staff member of another city department, on behalf a client, if the staff member is 
assigned or involved in a project that may potentially be brought before the Planning 
Commission (including but not limited to a staff member who advises or provides analysis to the 
Planning Commission regarding the project) and it is reasonably foreseeable that the Planning 
Commission's decision regarding the project in which the staff member is involved will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the commissioner's economic interests. 
1. Is a DSD employee involved in Planning Commission matters considered a "member, 
officer, employee, or consultant of the Planning Commission? 
Yes. A DSD employee involved in Planning Commission matters is considered a 
component of the Planning Commission itself. (Farrell Advice Letter, supra.) An appearance 
or communication before a DSD employee by a planning commissioner regarding a project that 
is or will be before the Planning Commission is considered an appearance or contact with the 
official's own agency. A planning commissioner is prohibited from making an appearance or 
communication before his or her own agency if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable 
material financial effect on any economic interest the official may have. 
2. 1',,fay a plam1}11g commissioner contact DSD clerical staff a:'isigned to the Planning 
Commission regarding a client's project? What if the communication is solely related to a 
ministerial issue? 
Yes, but only with regard to Process One projects or solely ministerial matters. It is 
foreseeable that Process Two through Five projects will come before the Planning Commission  
for review or a recommendation. An appearance or communication before DSD staff regarding 
any Process Two through Five projects will be considered an appearance or communication with 
the official's own agency. Accordingly, a planning commissioner is prohibited from making the 
appearance or communication if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on any economic interest the official may have. However, an appearance or 
communication before DSD staff regarding a Process One project, which will not come before 
the Planning Commission, is not an appearance or communication before an official's own 
agency. Accordingly, a planning commissioner may appear before or communicate with DSD 
staff regarding a Process One project so long as the commissioner does not purport to be acting 
on behalf of the Planning Commission. 
An official is not making or participating in making a government decision if the 
official's actions are solely ministerial, secretarial, manual, or clerical. (Regulation 
18702.4(a)(l).) We have also previously advised that a council member may contact city staff to 
"pose general questions which are ministerial in nature" (Torrance Advice Letter, No. A-94-084) 
and that "the Act would not prohibit [a planning commissioner] from obtaining information 
otherwise available to the public" (Gallagher Advice Letter, No. 1-94-279). Accordingly, a 
planning commissioner is not prohibited from communicating with a DSD clerical staff member, 
even if assigned to the Planning Commission or a project before the Planning Commission, if the 
matter is solely ministerial.3 
3. May a planning commissioner contact DSD employees regarding a client's Process 
One project? 
Yes. See response to Question 2. 
4. May a planning commissioner contact DSD employees regarding a client's Process 
Two or Three project? 
Yes, but only with regard to solely ministerial matters. See response to Question 2. 
5. Is a wSD Senior Planner considered a member, officer, employee, or consultant of the 
Planning Commission if the planner serves as a hearing officer for a Process Three project that 
may be appealed to the Planning Commission? 
Yes. A DSD Senior Planner that serves as a hearing officer for a Process Three project is 
considered a component of the Planning Commission itself. (Farrell Advice Letter, supra.) An 
appearance or communication by a planning commissioner regarding a Process Three Project, 
which may be appealed'lo the Planning Commission, before a DSD Senior Planner that serves as 
a hearing officer for the project is considered an appearance or contact with the planning 
commissioner's own agency. A planning commissioner is prohibited from making the 
3 If you need additional assistance delermining whether any particular communication is ministerial for 
purposes of the Act, you should seek additional advice providing a descriplion of the actual communication.  
appearance or communication if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on any economic interest the official may have. 
6. May a planning commissioner represent or assist a client at a Process Three project 
hearing in which a DSD Senior Planner is serving as the hearing officer and the city is 
represented by a DSD project manager who may latter be involved in the matter if appealed to 
the Planning Commission? 
No. An appearance or communication by a planning commissioner regarding a Process 
Three Project. which may be appealed to the Planning Commission, before a DSD Senior 
Planner that serves as a hearing officer for the project or a DSD project manager representing the 
city at the hearing is considered an appearance or contact with the planning commissioner's own 
agency. A planning commissioner is prohibited from making the appearance or communication 
if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic 
interest the official may have. 
7. May a planning commissioner contact a DSD employee regarding a client's project 
after the Planning Commission has acted on the project and there is no possibility that the 
matter will be remanded back to the Planning Commission? 
Yes. In the Farrell Advice Letter, supra, we previously found that a member of a design 
review board may communicate with shared city staff assigned to a project once the board had 
rendered is final design review for the project. We reached this conclusion because an official is 
not attempting to influence a decision by the official's agency if the official contacts shared city 
staff after the official's agency has made a final decision. Similarly, a planning commissioner is 
not attempting to influence the Planning Commission if the commissioner contacts a DSD 
employee assigned to the project after the Planning Commission renders its final decision 
regarding the project. Accordingly, once the Planning Commission has rendered its final 
decision regarding a project, a planning commissioner may contact a DSD employee regarding 
the project, even if the DSD employee is assigned to the project, so long as the commissioner 
does not purport to be acting on behalf of the Planning Commission. 
8. May a planning commissioner contact a DSD employee regarding a client's project if 
the employee will not be involved in presenting that particular project to the Planning 
Commission? Will the answer change if that employee is involved in presenting other projects to 
the Planning Commission? 
No. An appearance or communication by a planning commissioner regarding a eroject 
that is or will be before the Planning Commission to a DSD employee is considered an 
appearance or contact with the official's own agency because the DSD employee is considered 
shared staff for that particular project, even if the DSD employee will not be presenting the 
project to the Planning Commission. A planning commissioner is prohibited from making the 
appearance or communication if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on any economic interest the official may have.  
9. ls a matter before a CPG, which may be appealed to the Planning Commission, a 
governmental decision that is within or before the Planning Commission? 
Yes. CPGs are recognized by the City and advise the City on land use issues. 
Notwithstanding the fact that you have identified CPGs as private organizations, should 
members of a CPG make, participate in making, or use their official positions to influence 
governmental decisions, a CPG is an agency of the City. Conversely, if members of a CPG do 
not make. participate in making or use their official positions to influence governmental 
decisions, a CPG may be considered a purely advisory body. (See Barker Advice Letter. No. A-
1 1-241.) 
While you have not provided sufficient information for us to determine whether any 
particular CPG is a decision-making agency of the City or purely an advisory body, we find it 
unnecessary to make this determination at this time. Even if a CPG is acting only as an advisory 
body, CPG members or staff involved in making a recommendation regarding a project that may 
come before the Planning Commission must be considered shared staff of the Planning 
Commission. Thus, any appearance or communication before a CPG by a planning 
commissioner regarding a project that may come before the Planning Commission would be 
prohibited as an appearance or communication before the commissioner's own agency if the 
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interest the 
official may have. 
10. May a planning commissioner make a presentation concerning a client's project to a 
CPG if the CPG may make a recommendation on the project to the Planning Commission? 
No. See response to Question 9. 
I I. If a planning commissioner can address a CPG on behalf of a client, may the 
Planning Commission consider the minutes of the CPG meeting when reviewing the project? 
See response to Question 9. 
12. May a planning commissioner have discussions with DSD staff on behalf of a client 
regarding early feasibility issues if the permit process for the project has yet to be determined? 
No. Considering the Planning Commission's role in the Process Two through Five 
projects, it is foreseeable that projects for which the permit process has yet to be determined will 
eventually come before the Planning Commission. Thus, any appearance or communication by a 
planning commissioner before DSD staff [garding a project for which the permit process has yet 
to be determined would be prohibited as an appearance or communication before the 
commissioner's own agency if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on any economic interest the official may have. 


...  
13. May a planning commissioner make a presentation conceming a client's project to a 
different City board if that board also receives staff support from DSD? 
It depends. If the board is considering a project that may come before the Planning 
Commission, any appearance or communication by a planning commissioner before board staff 
regarding the project would be prohibited as an appearance or communication before the 
commissioner's own agency if the decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial 
effect on any economic interest the 01'.ficial may have. However, if the project will not come 
before the Planning Commission, a planning commissioner may make a presentation concerning 
a client's project, even if the board receives staff support from the DSD, so long as the 
commissioner does not purport to be acting on behalf of the Planning Commission. 
14. May a planning commissioner contact a DSD employee concerning a client's project 
that will be considered by a different city board if that DSD employee also works on Planning 
Commission matters? 
It depends. If the project may come before the Planning Commission, any appearance or 
communication by a planning commissioner before a DSD employee regarding the project would 
be prohibited as an appearance or communication before the commissioner's own agency if the 
decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on any economic interest the 
official may have. If the project will not come before the Planning Commission, a planning 
commissioner may contact a DSD employee concerning a project that will be considered by a 
different city board, even if the DSD employee also works on Pianning Commission matters, so 
long as the commissioner does not purport to be acting on behalf of the Planning Commission. 
15. For Questions 13 and 14, is it relevant whether the project under consideration by 
the other board could potentially come before the Planning Commission? 
Yes. See response to Questions 13 and 14.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May a planning commissioner contact other city employees regarding a land use decision 
that may potentially come before the Planning Commission?2 Informal assistance does not provide the rcquestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal 
written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c}(3).)

CONCLUSION: Generally, a planning commissioner may not appear before or communicate with a staff 
member of another city department, on behalf of a client, if the staff member is assigned or 
involved in a project that is or will be brought before the Planning Commission, and it is 
reasonably foreseeable that the Planning Commission's decision regarding the project will have a 
material financial effect on one or more of the commissioner's economic interests.
embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street ♦ Suite 620 ♦ Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 ♦ Fax (916) 322-0886 May 24, 2012 City of San Diego Ethics Committee Stacey Fulhorst Executive Director 1010 Second Avenue, Suite 1530 San Diego, CA 92101 Re: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-12-045 Dear Ms. Fulhorst: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission ("the Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when it renders assistance. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Because your question is general in nature and not limited to a specific governmental decision, we are treating your request as one for informal assistance.² Please note that the Commission does not provide advice on bodies of law outside the confines of the Act. Thus, we offer no opinion on the application of other incompatible activities and conflict-of-interest laws that may apply including, but not limited to, common law conflict of interest and Government Code Section 1090. QUESTION May a planning commissioner contact other city employees regarding a land use decision that may potentially come before the Planning Commission? ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. ² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).) File No. I-12-045 Page No. 2 CONCLUSION Generally, a planning commissioner may not appear before or communicate with a staff member of another city department, on behalf of a client, if the staff member is assigned or involved in a project that is or will be brought before the Planning Commission, and it is reasonably foreseeable that the Planning Commission's decision regarding the project will have a material financial effect on one or more of the commissioner's economic interests. FACTS You are the Executive Director for the City of San Diego Ethics Commission and seek general assistance regarding the Act's conflict-of-interest provision as applied to communications or appearances by planning commissioners before other city staff, including staff assigned to the Planning Commission, regarding projects that may come before the Planning Commission. You have provided the following facts for our consideration: • The Development Services Department (the "DSD") manages and oversees development projects in the City of San Diego (the "City"). • The DSD provides clerical support to the City's Planning Commission, and DSD managerial employees (including both Project Managers and Planners) attend Planning Commission meetings and hearings. • The Planning Commission does not have appointment or budgetary control over the DSD or
